scenario_name: Scenario 21 — Multi-Parcel Tracking Partial Delivery Detection
justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Shipment vs Parcel clearly distinguished as aggregate root and child entity with independent lifecycles and tracking numbers. (2) BR-2 explicitly states shipment status MUST be derived/computed from parcel statuses, never set independently — the critical domain insight is front and center. (3) Eight parcel-level states defined (Created, In Transit, Out for Delivery, Delivered, Failed Delivery Attempt, Lost, Returned, Held). (4) Business invariants thoroughly cover partial delivery detection (BR-3, BR-4), lost parcel escalation (BR-5), and parcel-level dispute granularity (BR-7). (5) Eight non-trivial edge cases surfaced including concurrent updates/race conditions, lost-then-disputed parcels, multi-carrier shipments, customs holds, and refused partial delivery. (6) Ten detailed acceptance criteria in Given/When/Then format.

observations.text: BR-2 nails the critical derivation rule — shipment status is explicitly called out as computed, never independently set, with a testable assertion that no API or method directly sets it.
Ubiquitous language section is exceptionally well-defined with 10 terms, grounding the analysis in logistics domain terminology rather than generic DDD jargon.

Edge cases go beyond the expected list — customs holds, refused partial delivery, dead-letter for unknown tracking numbers, and backward compatibility with single-parcel legacy shipments are all logistics-specific and non-obvious.
Acceptance criteria include a migration scenario (AC-8) converting legacy 1:1 shipments to the new model, which shows awareness of real-world adoption concerns.
Business impact analysis covers financial, operational, user, and risk dimensions with specific stakeholder groups identified — goes well beyond what the rubric requires.
State machine enforcement is codified as an invariant (BR-6) with explicit exception paths (Created → Lost for warehouse loss), showing nuanced understanding of logistics reality.

No explicit state machine diagram or transition table — states are listed in the ubiquitous language but valid transitions between them are only partially specified (BR-6 mentions the constraint but doesn't enumerate all valid transitions).

Refund/reshipment logic at parcel granularity is implied by BR-5 and BR-7 but not explicitly called out as a business rule — the claims workflow is mentioned but the specific mechanics of parcel-level refund vs reshipment are left to low-confidence areas.

agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Refactoring a logistics platform from shipment-level tracking to parcel-level tracking to enable partial delivery detection. Currently the system treats a shipment as a single atomic unit with one tracking number and one status. The business requires individual parcel lifecycle management so that scenarios like partial delivery, individual parcel loss, and customer disputes on partial receipt can be properly handled.","bounded_context":"Shipment & Parcel T
